content.claim: Academic selection via 6/8-year gimnázium tracks operates on top of and interacts with pre-existing ethnic and Roma school segregation, with segregation between Hungarian schools strengthening substantially between 1980 and 2011 and local school choice policies showing segregationist tendencies; attendance at high-poverty schools is associated with lower reading scores and lower likelihood of completing secondary education.
content.context: Fresh live research through the Equity and social mobility lens
limitations: The specific causal link from 6/8-year gimnázium admission to Roma/ethnic segregation is inferential and compositional rather than measured in a single study directly assessing gimnázium admission's ethnic effects
Kertesi & Kézdi report noise in data between 2006-2008, preventing firm conclusions about segregation trends in certain periods
Hermann & Kisfalusi's high-poverty school effects do not isolate the contribution of early gimnázium selection versus residential segregation and general vocational track placement
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
